text: Weil identified a specific failure mode she called 'imaginary attention' — the state of appearing to study or listen while the mind fills the void with fantasy and self-concern. This is distinct from mere distraction; it is worse because it produces the illusion of engagement. The student who reads a page while inwardly rehearsing a conversation, or the friend who nods while composing a reply — both have substituted a private theater for genuine contact with what is before them. Weil argued that imaginary attention is not a lesser version of real attention but its opposite: it actively prevents the self-emptying on which real understanding depends.
question: Why does Weil consider 'imaginary attention' worse than mere distraction?

options.text: It consumes more cognitive energy than genuine engagement
It produces the illusion of engagement while actively preventing real self-emptying
It is a conscious form of self-deception, whereas distraction is involuntary
It is more difficult to correct once it becomes habitual
